_meta.model: claude-sonnet-4-6; cut max_tokens = min(8000, 1200+220/min) + fill-the-time rule (±15% of target words)
_meta.how_to_regress: npm run check:golden the-runthrough  (dev backend up)
_meta.note: cut case guards honest fit-to-time sizing (was a "2-4 sentences" stub, then over-cutting).

output.trimmed_content:
This quarter taught us a lot — and I want to be honest about both the wins and the hard lessons, because that's how we get better together.

On enterprise revenue: we improved substantially, and I'll tell you exactly why — we invested early in the underlying data pipeline. That decision paid off. Similarly, our win rates against Competitor X created unexpected momentum, driven by better handoffs between sales and onboarding. When the process is right, results follow.

But not everything went smoothly. Self-serve signups stalled for six weeks — six weeks — until we finally addressed how regional teams share leads. That's a coordination failure we should have caught sooner, and we're fixing the structure so it doesn't happen again.

Churn in the SMB tier surprised everyone — in a good way. It outperformed projections after our capacity planning process kicked in. That's a lesson in trusting the work you put in upstream.

The pricing experiment was harder. It required three separate interventions, starting with our customer success playbook. That's not a clean story, but it's an honest one. We learned that pricing changes don't fail at the pricing level — they fail at the communication level. The mobile app rewrite exposed exactly the same gap: we don't always tell users what's changing and why. That's something we're correcting directly in the Q3 roadmap.

Onboarding completion rates taught us a hard lesson about our assumptions regarding user intent. We built for the user we imagined, not the user we have. European expansion, meanwhile, created unexpected momentum — but only after we tightened the handoff between sales and onboarding. Same root cause. Different surface.

On the infrastructure side, our decision to prioritize the feedback loop with early adopters validated itself this quarter. Support ticket volume confirmed it. The annual contract renewals process was messier — it stalled for six weeks while we worked through how regional teams were sharing lead context. Fixed now, but we lost time.

API adoption by integrators taught us the same lesson onboarding did: assumptions about user intent are expensive when they're wrong. The documentation overhaul validated our instinct to prioritize early adopter feedback — we're seeing that payoff now.

The February outage deserves a direct word. We treated it as a feedback event, not just an incident. That framing changed what we did next, and the data since then backs that up.

Hiring in engineering is stabilizing. The security audit surprised us — it outperformed projections, which is a credit to the team's preparation. The customer advisory board pushed us hard, and we needed three interventions to get the response right. That process made our Q3 roadmap sharper.

Here's the thread I want you to walk out of here with: the wins this quarter came from early investment — in pipelines, in process, in feedback loops. The misses came from assumptions we didn't pressure-test. Going into Q3, we're doing less assuming and more verifying. That's the standard now.

what_was_cut.section: Cyclical repetition of all 26 topics appearing two and three times each
what_was_cut.reason: The source rotates through the same topics with shuffled predicates in a pattern that reads as filler — collapsing each topic to one canonical appearance eliminates redundancy without losing any distinct insight.

what_was_cut.section: Second and third appearances of enterprise revenue, self-serve signups, churn in SMB tier, and pricing experiment
what_was_cut.reason: These topics were fully covered in their first pass; later appearances added no new information and would have caused the audience to disengage or distrust the structure of the presentation.

what_was_cut.section: Second and third appearances of European expansion, Delta Corp partnership, hiring, and security audit
what_was_cut.reason: Repeating these with different predicates contradicted the first statement for each topic, creating confusion about what actually happened rather than building a coherent narrative.

what_was_cut.section: Freemium conversion funnel and design system migration late-cycle repetitions
what_was_cut.reason: These appeared only in their rotated duplicate form and were cut in favor of topics with clearer cause-and-effect stories that support the closing argument.

what_was_cut.section: Referral program pilot and seasonal demand patterns
what_was_cut.reason: These topics appeared only with vague or recycled predicates and contributed no distinguishable insight to the core narrative about investment, process failure, and assumption-testing.

output.what_was_kept: Every distinct business insight in the source survived — wins tied to early investment, stalls tied to coordination failures, and hard lessons about assumption-testing — unified under a single closing argument the audience can act on.
output.pacing_notes: Slow down and hold a beat after 'six weeks — six weeks —' to let the self-serve failure land before moving on. Pause fully after 'we built for the user we imagined, not the user we have' — that's the sharpest line and the room needs a moment with it. Speed through the rapid-fire infrastructure and audit updates in the middle third to signal those are confirmations, not revelations, then slow back down for the closing paragraph so the final standard-setting line hits with weight.
